1: A revelation of God: 'the LORD is my light and my salvation; whom shall I fear? The LORD is the stronghold of my life; of whom shall I be afraid?' The threefold identification: light, salvation, stronghold. Jesus claims all three: 'I am the light of the world' (John 8:12; Psalms 9:5); his name means salvation (Matthew 1:21); he is the stronghold for those who take refuge in him (Psalms 2:12; Psalms 18:2). The fearlessness of Psalms 27:1 is the fearlessness Christ both embodies and imparts: 'Do not be afraid' is his consistent word (Matthew 14:27; Psalms 28:10; Revelation 1:17).
2: A shadow: 'when evildoers assail me to eat up my flesh, my adversaries and foes, it is they who stumble and fall.' The adversaries who 'eat up flesh' — the violent opposition that falls on itself. At the arrest in Gethsemane, those who came to take Jesus 'drew back and fell to the ground' (John 18:6) when Jesus identified himself — a momentary preview of Psalms 27:2's promise. The ultimate falling of the adversaries is the defeat of all opposition in the resurrection.
3: A shadow: 'though an army encamp against me, my heart shall not fear; though war arise against me, yet I will be confident.' The fearlessness before military opposition — the confidence that is not bravado but trust. Jesus in Gethsemane when 'a great crowd came... with swords and clubs' (Matthew 26:47): no fear, no flight, only the forward-moving trust: 'Rise, let us be going' (Matthew 26:46). The confidence of Psalms 27:3 is the Gethsemane calm of the one who trusts the Father's path.
4: A revelation of God: 'one thing have I asked of the LORD, that will I seek after: that I may dwell in the house of the LORD all the days of my life, to gaze upon the beauty of the LORD and to inquire in his temple.' The singular focus — to dwell with YHWH and behold his beauty. Christ's prayer in John 17:24: 'Father, I desire that they also... may be with me where I am, to see my glory that you have given me.' The gazing upon the divine beauty that Psalms 27:4 seeks is what Christ promises to his own: the beatific vision, the eternal dwelling with YHWH begun now and completed in the resurrection age.
5: A revelation of God: 'for he will hide me in his shelter in the day of trouble; he will conceal me under the cover of his tent; he will lift me high upon a rock.' The divine hiding and lifting — the protection of the Father for the Son in the 'day of trouble' (the crucifixion). The Father did not abandon the Son to permanent death but 'lifted him high' in the resurrection and ascension: 'God has highly exalted him' (Philippians 2:9). The rock upon which he is set is the resurrection victory.
6: A theme: 'and now my head shall be lifted up above my enemies all around me, and I will offer in his tent sacrifices with shouts of joy; I will sing and make melody to the LORD.' 'My head lifted above my enemies' — the resurrection reversal: the head bowed in death is lifted in vindication. The 'sacrifices with shouts of joy' anticipate the Eucharist — the todah meal offered in the tent of the new covenant by the risen Christ through his community.
7: A revelation of God: 'hear, O LORD, when I cry aloud; be gracious to me and answer me!' The petition for the divine hearing. Hebrews 5:7: 'In the days of his flesh, Jesus offered up prayers and supplications, with loud cries and tears, to him who was able to save him from death, and he was heard because of his reverence.' The cry of Psalms 27:7 is answered in Christ's resurrection: YHWH heard and answered — and the answer was the empty tomb.
8: A revelation of God: 'you have said, Seek my face. My heart says to you, Your face, LORD, I seek.' The divine command and the human response — the dialogue of seeking. The face of YHWH that the whole OT sought to see is now available in Christ: 'No one has ever seen God; the only God, who is at the Father's side, he has made him known' (John 1:18). 'The light of the knowledge of the glory of God in the face of Jesus Christ' (2 Corinthians 4:6) — the face YHWH said to seek has been found in the incarnation.
9: A shadow: 'hide not your face from me. Turn not your servant away in anger, O you who have been my help. Cast me not off; forsake me not, O God of my salvation!' The dread of divine face-hiding — the counterpart of Psalms 22:1 ('why have you forsaken me?'). Christ entered the face-hiding at its maximum depth so that it would not be the final word for any who trust him. The 'do not forsake me' of Psalms 27:9 is answered by the resurrection-promise: 'I am with you always, to the end of the age' (Matthew 28:20).
10: A revelation of God: 'for my father and my mother have forsaken me, but the LORD will take me in.' The abandonment of the closest human bonds — and the divine adoption that supersedes them. The cross was the moment of maximum human abandonment: 'they all left him and fled' (Matthew 26:56). Yet the Father 'took him in' in the resurrection. For those who are forsaken, Christ promises the same adoption: 'I will not leave you as orphans; I will come to you' (John 14:18).
11: A theme: 'teach me your way, O LORD, and lead me on a level path because of my enemies.' 'Teach me your way' — the discipleship prayer. Christ is both the teacher of the way (the Sermon on the Mount, the parables) and the way itself (John 14:6). The 'level path' despite enemies is the path Christ walked to the cross and through it — the level path that is not detoured by opposition but goes straight through it.
12: A shadow: 'give me not up to the will of my adversaries; for false witnesses have risen against me, and they breathe out violence.' 'False witnesses have risen against me' — the trial before the Sanhedrin: 'the chief priests and the whole council were seeking false testimony against Jesus that they might put him to death, but they found none, though many false witnesses came forward' (Matthew 26:59-60). The false witnesses of Psalms 27:12 are the false witnesses at Jesus's trial.
13: A revelation of God: 'I believe that I shall look upon the goodness of the LORD in the land of the living!' The confident faith in divine goodness — the resurrection hope before the resurrection happens. 'The land of the living' — the realm of life beyond death: Christ's resurrection establishes that the land of the living is not a metaphor but a destination. 'I am the resurrection and the life' (John 11:25): the land of the living is where Christ dwells, and he invites his own to join him there.
14: A revelation of God: 'wait for the LORD; be strong, and let your heart take courage; wait for the LORD!' The closing summons — wait, take courage, wait again. The Advent posture: the community that waits for Christ's return. 'Wait for the LORD' is the posture between Easter and the Parousia — courage sustained by the certainty that the God who raised Jesus will complete what he has begun. The psalm that began with fearlessness (v1: 'whom shall I fear?') ends with courageous waiting — the two poles of the Christian life.
